text: We describe in this video the G-pouch microvascularization assessment using the knee infrared technology. We will perform three different assessments. The first one after we're building the pouch, the second after the elongation procedure if needed, and the final product at the end of the surgery after the G-pouch has been performed.

The surgeon uses a near-infrared scope and injects three milliliters of indocyanine green intravenously. The screen displays three squares on the left: a normal view, a black and white near-infrared view, and an enhanced reality view. The large screen can be adjusted to show any of these views.

The confection of a G-pouch after coloprotectomy is of major importance for the future function of the pouch. Therefore, we think it is valuable to have a look at the microvascularization of the pouch.

The surgeon frees the small bowel mesentery to maximize its length, preparing for the assessment of the pouch's microvascularization. In cases requiring additional length for anastomosis, the surgeon clamps the vascular supply of the pouch using a vascular clamp.

The screen displays three views: normal light, infrared light, and enhanced reality. The black and white infrared view confirms that the vascular supply to the bottom end of the pouch is preserved, even after the elongation procedure.

The surgeon divides the vessels, allowing the pouch to be safely positioned at the bottom end for anastomosis. After completing the anastomosis, the intra-abdominal view shows the joint at the top end. Upon injecting endocyanin green, the microvessels are visibly highlighted, indicating successful vascularization up to the anastomosis site.

The surgeon performs an endoanal view of the G-pouch, inspecting the interior for any signs of bleeding and assessing the vascularization. Once confirmed to be well vascularized, the procedure can proceed to the ileostomy.

The surgeon inspects the stable line of the G-pouch for any signs of bleeding. If bleeding is observed, it is localized and stitched to ensure hemostasis. Once confirmed that there is no bleeding, the operation can be safely concluded.
In conclusion, the use of an ear infrared scope in GPOW surgery is highly feasible and adds safety about the patch blood supply at the time of surgery.
